methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $36M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Biotech & Health. Range: $100M - $3.3B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Biotech & Health P/S median is 7.8x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Biotech & Health

private.name: Zeta Surgical
private.rationale: Same sector (Biotech & Health), same stage (Series A), post-money $100M

data.narrative:
Living Carbon is a Series A biotech company that has raised $36M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of 8 private-stage peers and 3 public biotech firms in the Biotech & Health sector, including Series B-stage companies such as Eikon Therapeutics, Neko Health, New Limit, Asimov, and Profluent.

Two valuation methods are applicable. Stage-progression anchors to the $36M in total capital raised and applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage transitions, implying a $72M valuation. Peer-median triangulation uses 6 same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables with valuations ranging from $100M to $3.3B; the median across this set is $805M. Public-comp anchoring cannot be applied because Living Carbon's revenue is not disclosed, limiting the utility of the 7.8x price-to-sales median observed across three public biotech tickers (RXRX, TEM, ONT.L).

The triangulated valuation range is $61M (low) to $926M (high), with a median of $438M. This wide dispersion reflects the substantial variance in peer valuations and the structural gap between the stage-progression method and peer medians. Key uncertainties include the company's revenue trajectory, competitive differentiation relative to the peer set, and whether early-stage funding multiples or later-stage comparable valuations better reflect current risk.
